# Review Math

Assume the role of a PhD-level mathematician.

The goal is to review a particular Erdos Problem formalization (problem number NUM) to answer the following questions. Produce a review document called ai-review/NUM.md.

1. Code reuse - Can any code from the existing codebase be repurposed? Look in FormalConjecturesForMathlib to determine if an existing implementation would work just as well.
2. Citations - Fetch data from https://www.erdosproblems.com/NUM to ensure any citations included in docstrings are documented as they exist on the website as opposed to shorthand references.
3. Variants - Are all variants of the problem captured by the formalization?
4. Readability - Could the code be made more readable?
5. Formalizability - Is the problem as stated precise enough to be obviously formalizable? Provide an assessment of the ambiguity of the statement.
6. Correctness - Is the formalization as-implemented correct and complete from a mathematical perspective? Would an experienced mathematician identify any obvious flaws? Is any incompleteness or incorrectness attributable to ambiguity in the statement itself?

    18Ôćĺ
    19Ôćĺ/-!
    20Ôćĺ# Erd┼Ĺs Problem 106
    21Ôćĺ
    22Ôćĺ*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/106](https://www.erdosproblems.com/106)
    23Ôćĺ
    24ÔćĺDraw $n$ non-overlapping squares (possibly rotated) inside the unit square. Let $f(n)$ be
    25Ôćĺthe maximum total side length. Is $f(k^2 + 1) = k$ for every positive integer $k$?
    26Ôćĺ-/
    27Ôćĺ
    28Ôćĺopen BigOperators Real
    29Ôćĺ
    30Ôćĺnamespace Erdos106
    31Ôćĺ
    32Ôćĺ/--
    33ÔćĺA square placement in $\mathbb{R}^2$: a center point, a positive side length, and a
    34Ôćĺrotation angle (in radians) measuring how far the square is rotated from
    35Ôćĺthe standard axis-aligned orientation.
    36Ôćĺ-/
    37Ôćĺstructure SquarePlacement where
    38Ôćĺ  center : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ
    39Ôćĺ  side   : ÔäŁ
    40Ôćĺ  angle  : ÔäŁ
    41Ôćĺ  side_pos : 0 < side
    42Ôćĺ
    43Ôćĺ/--
    44ÔćĺThe closed region occupied by a square placement. A point $p$ lies in the
    45Ôćĺregion iff, when translated so the center is at the origin and then rotated
    46Ôćĺby $-\mathrm{angle}$, its coordinates both lie in $[-\mathrm{side}/2, \mathrm{side}/2]$.
    47Ôćĺ-/
    48Ôćĺnoncomputable def SquarePlacement.region (sq : SquarePlacement) : Set (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
    49Ôćĺ  {p : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ |
    50Ôćĺ    let u :=  (p.1 - sq.center.1) * cos sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * sin sq.angle
    51Ôćĺ    let v := -(p.1 - sq.center.1) * sin sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * cos sq.angle
    52Ôćĺ    |u| ÔëĄ sq.side / 2 Ôłž |v| ÔëĄ sq.side / 2}
    53Ôćĺ
    54Ôćĺ/--
    55ÔćĺThe open interior of a square placement (strict inequalities).
    56Ôćĺ-/
    57Ôćĺnoncomputable def SquarePlacement.sqInterior (sq : SquarePlacement) : Set (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
    58Ôćĺ  {p : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ |
    59Ôćĺ    let u :=  (p.1 - sq.center.1) * cos sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * sin sq.angle
    60Ôćĺ    let v := -(p.1 - sq.center.1) * sin sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * cos sq.angle
    61Ôćĺ    |u| < sq.side / 2 Ôłž |v| < sq.side / 2}
    62Ôćĺ
    63Ôćĺ/--
    64ÔćĺThe unit square $[0,1]^2 \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$.
    65Ôćĺ-/
    66Ôćĺdef unitSquare : Set (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
    67Ôćĺ  {p : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ | 0 ÔëĄ p.1 Ôłž p.1 ÔëĄ 1 Ôłž 0 ÔëĄ p.2 Ôłž p.2 ÔëĄ 1}
    68Ôćĺ
    69Ôćĺ/--
    70ÔćĺA valid configuration of $n$ squares inside the unit square:
    71Ôćĺ1. Each square's closed region is contained in the unit square.
    72Ôćĺ2. No two distinct squares share an interior point (their open interiors
    73Ôćĺ   are disjoint).
    74Ôćĺ-/
    75Ôćĺdef IsValidSquareConfig (n : ÔäĽ) (config : Fin n Ôćĺ SquarePlacement) : Prop :=
    76Ôćĺ  (ÔłÇ i : Fin n, (config i).region ÔŐć unitSquare) Ôłž
    77Ôćĺ  (ÔłÇ i j : Fin n, i Ôëá j Ôćĺ Disjoint (config i).sqInterior (config j).sqInterior)
    78Ôćĺ
    79Ôćĺ/--
    80Ôćĺ$f(n)$ is the supremum of the total side-length sum over all valid configurations
    81Ôćĺof $n$ (possibly rotated) squares inside the unit square with pairwise disjoint
    82Ôćĺinteriors.
    83Ôćĺ-/
    84Ôćĺnoncomputable def f (n : ÔäĽ) : ÔäŁ :=
    85Ôćĺ  sSup {s : ÔäŁ | Ôłâ config : Fin n Ôćĺ SquarePlacement,
    86Ôćĺ    IsValidSquareConfig n config Ôłž s = ÔłĹ i : Fin n, (config i).side}
    87Ôćĺ
    88Ôćĺ/--
    89ÔćĺErd┼Ĺs Problem 106:
    90Ôćĺ
    91ÔćĺDraw $n$ squares (not necessarily axis-aligned) inside the unit square $[0,1]^2$
    92Ôćĺwith no two squares sharing a common interior point. Let $f(n)$ be the maximum
    93Ôćĺpossible sum of the side-lengths of the squares.
    94Ôćĺ
    95Ôćĺ**Conjecture**: $f(k^2 + 1) = k$ for every positive integer $k$.
    96Ôćĺ
    97ÔćĺBackground:
    98Ôćĺ- It follows easily from the CauchyÔÇôSchwarz inequality that $f(k^2) = k$.
    99Ôćĺ- The lower bound $f(k^2 + 1) \geq k$ is elementary: subdivide $[0,1]^2$ into $k^2$
   100Ôćĺ  squares of side $1/k$, then replace any one of them by two squares of side
   101Ôćĺ  $1/(2k)$; the total side-length is $(k^2 - 1)/k + 2 \cdot 1/(2k) = k$.
   102Ôćĺ- The conjecture asserts that this lower bound is tight: no configuration of
   103Ôćĺ  $k^2 + 1$ squares can exceed the total side-length $k$ achievable with $k^2$ squares.
   104Ôćĺ- Baek, Koizumi, and Ueoro (2024) proved the axis-aligned variant: if all
   105Ôćĺ  squares are required to have sides parallel to the coordinate axes, then the
   106Ôćĺ  supremum equals $k$.
   107Ôćĺ-/
   108Ôćĺ@[category research open, AMS 52]
   109Ôćĺtheorem erdos_106 :
   110Ôćĺ    answer(sorry) Ôćö ÔłÇ k : ÔäĽ, 0 < k Ôćĺ f (k ^ 2 + 1) = (k : ÔäŁ) := by
   111Ôćĺ  sorry
   112Ôćĺ
   113Ôćĺend Erdos106

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erd┼Ĺs Problem 106

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/106](https://www.erdosproblems.com/106)

Draw $n$ non-overlapping squares (possibly rotated) inside the unit square. Let $f(n)$ be
the maximum total side length. Is $f(k^2 + 1) = k$ for every positive integer $k$?
-/

open BigOperators Real

namespace Erdos106

/--
A square placement in $\mathbb{R}^2$: a center point, a positive side length, and a
rotation angle (in radians) measuring how far the square is rotated from
the standard axis-aligned orientation.
-/
structure SquarePlacement where
  center : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ
  side   : ÔäŁ
  angle  : ÔäŁ
  side_pos : 0 < side

/--
The closed region occupied by a square placement. A point $p$ lies in the
region iff, when translated so the center is at the origin and then rotated
by $-\mathrm{angle}$, its coordinates both lie in $[-\mathrm{side}/2, \mathrm{side}/2]$.
-/
noncomputable def SquarePlacement.region (sq : SquarePlacement) : Set (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
  {p : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ |
    let u :=  (p.1 - sq.center.1) * cos sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * sin sq.angle
    let v := -(p.1 - sq.center.1) * sin sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * cos sq.angle
    |u| ÔëĄ sq.side / 2 Ôłž |v| ÔëĄ sq.side / 2}

/--
The open interior of a square placement (strict inequalities).
-/
noncomputable def SquarePlacement.sqInterior (sq : SquarePlacement) : Set (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
  {p : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ |
    let u :=  (p.1 - sq.center.1) * cos sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * sin sq.angle
    let v := -(p.1 - sq.center.1) * sin sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * cos sq.angle
    |u| < sq.side / 2 Ôłž |v| < sq.side / 2}

/--
The unit square $[0,1]^2 \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$.
-/
def unitSquare : Set (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
  {p : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ | 0 ÔëĄ p.1 Ôłž p.1 ÔëĄ 1 Ôłž 0 ÔëĄ p.2 Ôłž p.2 ÔëĄ 1}

/--
A valid configuration of $n$ squares inside the unit square:
1. Each square's closed region is contained in the unit square.
2. No two distinct squares share an interior point (their open interiors
   are disjoint).
-/
def IsValidSquareConfig (n : ÔäĽ) (config : Fin n Ôćĺ SquarePlacement) : Prop :=
  (ÔłÇ i : Fin n, (config i).region ÔŐć unitSquare) Ôłž
  (ÔłÇ i j : Fin n, i Ôëá j Ôćĺ Disjoint (config i).sqInterior (config j).sqInterior)

/--

# Erd┼Ĺs Problem #106: Complete Information

## Problem Statement
Draw $n$ squares inside a unit square with no overlapping interiors. Let $f(n)$ denote the maximum possible sum of the side-lengths. The central conjecture asks: **Is $f(k^2+1)=k$?**

## Status
**OPEN** - Falsifiable through finite counterexample.

## Key Results & Findings

**Proven Facts:**
- $f(2)=1$ (Erd┼Ĺs, early paper)
- $f(5)=2$ (Newman, personal communication)
- $f(k^2)=k$ (trivial from Cauchy-Schwarz)
- $f(k^2+1) \geq k$ (constructive proof given)

**Recent Major Breakthrough:**
Baek, Koizumi, and Ueoro [BKU24] proved the restricted case where "all squares have sides parallel to the unit square's sides," establishing $g(k^2+1)=k$ and generalizing to $g(k^2+2c+1)=k+c/k$ for $-k<c<k$.

**Constructions (Hal├ísz [Ha84]):**
- $f(k^2+2c+1) \geq k+\frac{c}{k}$
- $f(k^2+2c) \geq k+\frac{c}{k+1}$

## References Cited
- [ErGr75b], [Er94b], [Er95] - Erd┼Ĺs sources
- Ha84 - Hal├ísz
- ErSo95 - Erd┼Ĺs & Soifer
- CaSt05 - Campbell & Staton
- Pr08 - Praton
- BKU24 - Baek, Koizumi, Ueoro
- Ra26 - Raj Singh

## Additional Context
- Erd┼Ĺs dated this conjecture to "more than 60 years ago"
- Secondary open question: for which $n$ is $f(n+1)=f(n)$?
- Praton proved the general conjecture is equivalent to $f(k^2+1)=k$
- Raj Singh demonstrated convergence equivalences

# AI Review: Erd┼Ĺs Problem 101

## 1. Code Reuse

**The definitions in `101.lean` are entirely subsumed by the generic versions in `588.lean`.**

- **`NoFiveCollinear`** (lines 34ÔÇô36): This is exactly `NoKPlusOneCollinear 4` from `588.lean:34ÔÇô36`. It is also equivalent to `NoKCollinear 5` from `960.lean:38ÔÇô40`. Three separate files define what is semantically the same predicate at different levels of generality. Problem 588 is explicitly described as the generalization of Problem 101 (with $k = 4$).

- **`fourRichLineCount`** (lines 45ÔÇô48): This is exactly `kRichLineCount 4` from `588.lean:42ÔÇô45`, and is identical (up to name) to `fourPlusRichLineCount` from `102.lean:35ÔÇô38`. All three definitions use the same construction: `Set.ncard` over the set of `AffineSubspace`s with `Module.finrank ÔäŁ L.direction = 1` and `k ÔëĄ Set.ncard {points on L}`.

**Recommendation:** Problem 101 should import and reuse the definitions from `588.lean` (or a shared utility). This would make the relationship between the problems explicit and reduce maintenance burden. Alternatively, the shared definitions (`kRichLineCount`, `NoKPlusOneCollinear`) could be extracted to a geometry utility module used by 101, 102, 588, and 960.

## 2. Citations

The formalization includes only a link to [erdosproblems.com/101](https://www.erdosproblems.com/101). The website lists the following information that should be documented:

**Related problems:**
- Problem #102 (Erd┼ĹsÔÇôPurdy conjecture: if $\geq cn^2$ four-rich lines, some line has unboundedly many points)
- Problem #588 (generalization to $k$-rich lines for $k \geq 4$)
- Problem #669

**Key references and constructions:**
- Gr├╝nbaum (1976): Constructed examples with $\gg n^{3/2}$ four-rich lines (no five collinear), leading Erd┼Ĺs to initially guess $n^{3/2}$ as the correct order
- Solymosi and Stojakovi─ç (2013): Constructed point sets with no five collinear and $\geq n^{2 - O(1/\sqrt{\log n})}$ lines containing exactly four points, disproving Erd┼Ĺs's $n^{3/2}$ guess and showing the bound is essentially tight

**Other metadata:**
- Listed as Problem 71 on Green's open problems list
- Related OEIS sequence: A006065
- Prize: $100

The docstring should mention at minimum the SolymosiÔÇôStojakovi─ç lower bound, as it is the state of the art and directly informs the tightness of the conjecture.

## 3. Variants

The formalization captures only the main conjecture: the number of four-rich lines is $o(n^2)$.

**Missing variants / related questions:**
- **Exact vs. at-least four:** The website describes lines containing "exactly four points," while the formalization counts lines with "at least four" points. Under the no-five-collinear hypothesis these are equivalent (no line can contain 5+ points), so the formalization is correct. However, the distinction should be noted in the docstring for clarity.
- **Quantitative bounds:** Erd┼Ĺs originally speculated the correct order was $n^{3/2}$, which was refuted by SolymosiÔÇôStojakovi─ç's $n^{2-O(1/\sqrt{\log n})}$ construction. A companion statement formalizing a known upper bound (if any exists) would provide useful context.
- **Connection to 588:** The formalization could note that `erdos_101` is the special case $k = 4$ of `erdos_588`, and that a proof of 588 would imply 101.

**Assessment:** The core conjecture is captured. The lack of an explicit link to the generalized Problem 588 is a missed opportunity for mathematical context.

## 4. Readability

The code is well-structured and readable:

- **Good:** Each definition has a clear docstring explaining the mathematical meaning.
- **Good:** The namespace `Erdos101` avoids name collisions.
- **Good:** The formal encoding of $o(n^2)$ as "for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that..." is clearly explained in the theorem docstring.
- **Minor:** The set comprehension in `fourRichLineCount` nests two `Set.ncard` calls and a conjunction, which is somewhat dense. This is an inherent complexity of the definition and is handled identically in 102, 588, and 960, so it follows established convention.
- **Minor:** The name `fourRichLineCount` is slightly inconsistent with `fourPlusRichLineCount` in 102.lean, even though they compute the same thing. Standardizing on one name (or reusing from 588) would improve consistency.

## 5. Formalizability

**Assessment: Highly formalizable. Low ambiguity.**

The problem statement is precise:
- "No five collinear" is a standard, well-defined geometric condition with a canonical Mathlib formalization via `Collinear ÔäŁ`.
- "Lines containing (at least/exactly) four points" is unambiguous once the collinearity constraint is in place.
- "$o(n^2)$" has a standard $\varepsilon$-$N$ formulation.

The only potential source of ambiguity ÔÇö "exactly four" vs. "at least four" ÔÇö is resolved by the no-five-collinear constraint, which ensures equivalence. The problem is unambiguously formalizable as stated.

## 6. Correctness

**The formalization is mathematically correct but has a structural concern regarding the open/closed status.**

Detailed analysis:

- **Encoding of $o(n^2)$:** The statement asserts: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that for all $n \geq N$ and every valid $n$-point configuration $P$, `fourRichLineCount P ÔëĄ ╬Á ┬Ě n┬▓`. This correctly encodes the assertion that the *maximum* number of four-rich lines over all valid $n$-point configurations is $o(n^2)$. The universal quantification over $P$ correctly captures the worst case. **Correct.**

- **`NoFiveCollinear`:** Quantifies over all 5-element subsets and asserts non-collinearity via Mathlib's `Collinear ÔäŁ`. This is the standard characterization of "no five points are collinear." **Correct.**

- **`fourRichLineCount`:** Counts `Set.ncard` of the set of 1-dimensional affine subspaces containing $\geq 4$ points of $P$. The dimension-1 constraint correctly identifies lines (as opposed to points or the whole plane). For finite $P$, the set of such lines is finite (bounded by $\binom{n}{2}$), so `Set.ncard` agrees with the true cardinality. **Correct.**

- **Open problem stated as a theorem:** Problem 101 is **open** (with a \$100 prize). The formalization states `erdos_101` as a direct `theorem ... := by sorry`, which asserts the conjecture is true. By contrast, Problem 588 (the generalization) uses the `answer(sorry) Ôćö ...` pattern, which correctly encodes an open yes/no question. This inconsistency is notable: if the conjecture turns out to be false (and SolymosiÔÇôStojakovi─ç's near-$n^2$ lower bound suggests this is not inconceivable), then `erdos_101` as stated would be *unprovable* (not merely unproven). The `answer(sorry) Ôćö` pattern used in 588.lean would be more appropriate here, since the problem asks to "determine whether" the count is $o(n^2)$, not to prove that it is.

  That said, other open conjectures in the codebase (e.g., 102, 960) also use the direct theorem pattern, suggesting this may be a deliberate project convention for problems where the expected answer is "yes." If so, this is acceptable but should be documented.

- **Equivalence with Problem 588 at $k=4$:** The statement of `erdos_101` is logically equivalent to `erdos_588` instantiated at $k = 4$ (with the forward direction of the `answer(sorry) Ôćö` in 588). Specifically, `NoFiveCollinear P` is definitionally equal to `NoKPlusOneCollinear 4 P`, and `fourRichLineCount P` equals `kRichLineCount 4 P`. **Correct.**

**Verdict:** The formalization is mathematically correct as a statement of the conjecture. The use of a direct theorem (rather than `answer(sorry) Ôćö`) for an open problem is a minor structural concern, particularly given that the generalization (588) uses the more cautious pattern.

## Summary

| Criterion | Rating | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Code reuse | Needs improvement | Both definitions are special cases of 588.lean's generic versions; 102.lean has an identical copy |
| Citations | Needs improvement | Missing references (Gr├╝nbaum 1976, SolymosiÔÇôStojakovi─ç 2013), related problems (102, 588, 669), and prize information |
| Variants | Acceptable | Core conjecture captured; could note equivalence of "exactly/at-least four" and connection to Problem 588 |
| Readability | Good | Well-documented, follows codebase conventions; minor naming inconsistency with 102.lean |
| Formalizability | Excellent | Unambiguous, precise problem statement |
| Correctness | Correct (with caveat) | Mathematically faithful; direct-theorem pattern for an open problem is inconsistent with the generalization (588) which uses `answer(sorry) Ôćö` |

   165ÔćĺDense packings of congruent circles in a circle. Discrete Math 1998;181:139ÔÇô154.
   166Ôćĺ-/
   167Ôćĺ@[category undergraduate, AMS 51]
   168Ôćĺtheorem fifteen_circle_packing_in_circle_bound :
   169Ôćĺ    Nonempty (Packing 15 UnitCircle (Circle (1 + ÔłÜ (6 + 2 / ÔłÜ 5 + 4 * ÔłÜ (1 + 2 / ÔłÜ 5)))) ) := by
   170Ôćĺ  sorry
   171Ôćĺ
   172Ôćĺ/--
   173ÔćĺWhat is the smallest circle that can contain 15 unit circles?
   174Ôćĺ
   175ÔćĺReference:
   176ÔćĺGraham RL, Lubachevsky BD, Nurmela KJ, Ostergard PRJ.
   177ÔćĺDense packings of congruent circles in a circle. Discrete Math 1998;181:139ÔÇô154.
   178Ôćĺ-/
   179Ôćĺ@[category research open, AMS 51]
   180Ôćĺtheorem least_fifteen_circle_packing_in_circle :
   181Ôćĺ    IsLeast {r : ÔäŁ | Nonempty (Packing 15 UnitCircle (Circle r))} answer(sorry) := by

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Packing

This file contains a number of open problems related to the minimal size of a square (or circle)
that can contain a given number of unit squares (or circles).
In each case, we provide a known upper bound, and ask for the least such size.

*References:*
- [Wikipedia on packing of squares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_packing)
- [Wikipedia on packing of circles in a circle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circle_packing_in_a_circle)
- [Wikipedia on packing of circles in a square](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circle_packing_in_a_square)
- Friedman, Erich (2009), "Packing unit squares in squares: a survey and new results",
  Electronic Journal of Combinatorics, 1000, Dynamic Survey 7
- A website with visualizations of packings:
  [link](https://erich-friedman.github.io/packing/)
-/

open EuclideanGeometry

universe u

namespace SquarePacking

/--
A square of a particular side length as a subset of the Euclidean plane.
Not including border, so that squares that touch at the border are disjoint,
but a square internal to another shape is a subset of that shape.
-/
def Square (side : ÔäŁ) : Set ÔäŁ┬▓ :=
  {p : ÔäŁ┬▓ | 0 < p 0 Ôłž p 0 < side Ôłž 0 < p 1 Ôłž p 1 < side}

/--
The unit square as a subset of the Euclidean plane.
-/
def UnitSquare : Set ÔäŁ┬▓ := Square 1

/--
A circle of a particular radius as a subset of the Euclidean plane.
Not including border, so that circles that touch at the border are disjoint,
but a circle internal to another shape is a subset of that shape.
-/
def Circle (r : ÔäŁ) : Set ÔäŁ┬▓ :=
  {p : ÔäŁ┬▓ | p 0 ^ 2 + p 1 ^ 2 < r ^ 2}

/--
The unit circle as a subset of the Euclidean plane.
-/
def UnitCircle : Set ÔäŁ┬▓ := Circle 1

/--
A structure representing a packing of `n` isometric embeddings
of a set `s` inside a (presumably larger) set `S`.
-/
structure Packing (n : ÔäĽ) (s : Set ÔäŁ┬▓) (S : Set ÔäŁ┬▓) where
  /-- The isometric equivalences
  that represent the transformations of the base shape to their locations in the packing. -/
  embeddings : Fin n Ôćĺ (ÔäŁ┬▓ ÔëâßÁó ÔäŁ┬▓)
  /-- The images of the embeddings are pairwise disjoint -/
  disjoint : Pairwise fun i j => Disjoint (embeddings i '' s) (embeddings j '' s)
  /-- The images of the embeddings are all inside the larger set `S` -/
  inside : ÔłÇ i : Fin n, embeddings i '' s ÔŐć S

/--
Eleven unit squares can be packed into a square of side length < 3.877084.

Reference: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_packing#In_a_square)
-/
@[category undergraduate, AMS 51]
theorem eleven_square_packing_in_square_bound :
    Nonempty (Packing 11 UnitSquare (Square 3.877084)) := by
  sorry

/--
What is the smallest square that can contain 11 unit squares?

Reference: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_packing#In_a_square)
-/
@[category research open, AMS 51]
theorem least_eleven_square_packing_in_square :
    IsLeast {x : ÔäŁ | Nonempty (Packing 11 UnitSquare (Square x))} answer(sorry) := by
  sorry

/--
Seventeen unit squares can be packed into a square of side length < 4.6756.

Reference: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_packing#In_a_square)
-/
@[category undergraduate, AMS 51]
theorem seventeen_square_packing_in_square_bound :
    Nonempty (Packing 17 UnitSquare (Square 4.6756)) := by
  sorry

/--
What is the smallest square that can contain 17 unit squares?

Reference: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_packing#In_a_square)
-/
@[category research open, AMS 51]
theorem least_seventeen_square_packing_in_square :
    IsLeast {x : ÔäŁ | Nonempty (Packing 17 UnitSquare (Square x))} answer(sorry) := by
  sorry

/--
Three unit squares can be packed into a circle of radius $(5 \sqrt{17}) / 16 \approx 1.288$.

Reference: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_packing#In_a_circle)
-/
@[category undergraduate, AMS 51]
theorem three_square_packing_in_circle_bound :
    Nonempty (Packing 3 UnitSquare (Circle ((5 * ÔłÜ 17) / 16))) := by
  sorry

/--
What is the smallest circle that can contain 3 unit squares?

Reference: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_packing#In_a_circle)
-/
@[category research open, AMS 51]
theorem least_three_square_packing_in_circle :
    IsLeast {r : ÔäŁ | Nonempty (Packing 3 UnitSquare (Circle r))} answer(sorry) := by
  sorry

/--
Twenty-one unit circles can be packed into a square of side length < 9.359.

Reference: [Visualizations](https://erich-friedman.github.io/packing/cirinsqu/)
-/
@[category undergraduate, AMS 51]
theorem twenty_one_circle_packing_in_square_bound :
    Nonempty (Packing 21 UnitCircle (Square 9.359)) := by
  sorry

/--
What is the smallest square that can contain 21 unit circles?
-/
@[category research open, AMS 51]
theorem least_twenty_one_circle_packing_in_square :
    IsLeast {x : ÔäŁ | Nonempty (Packing 21 UnitCircle (Square x))} answer(sorry) := by
  sorry

/--
Fifteen unit circles can be packed into a circle of radius
$1 + \sqrt{6 + 2/\sqrt{5} + 4 \sqrt{1 + 2/\sqrt{5}}} \approx 4.521$.

Reference:
Graham RL, Lubachevsky BD, Nurmela KJ, Ostergard PRJ.
Dense packings of congruent circles in a circle. Discrete Math 1998;181:139ÔÇô154.
-/
@[category undergraduate, AMS 51]
theorem fifteen_circle_packing_in_circle_bound :
    Nonempty (Packing 15 UnitCircle (Circle (1 + ÔłÜ (6 + 2 / ÔłÜ 5 + 4 * ÔłÜ (1 + 2 / ÔłÜ 5)))) ) := by
  sorry

/--
What is the smallest circle that can contain 15 unit circles?

Reference:
Graham RL, Lubachevsky BD, Nurmela KJ, Ostergard PRJ.
Dense packings of congruent circles in a circle. Discrete Math 1998;181:139ÔÇô154.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 51]
theorem least_fifteen_circle_packing_in_circle :
    IsLeast {r : ÔäŁ | Nonempty (Packing 15 UnitCircle (Circle r))} answer(sorry) := by
  sorry

end SquarePacking

    16Ôćĺ
    17Ôćĺimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18Ôćĺ
    19Ôćĺopen Real MeasureTheory Measure Module
    20Ôćĺ
    21Ôćĺ/-!
    22Ôćĺ# Mathoverflow 34145
    23Ôćĺ
    24ÔćĺCan the unit square be covered by $1/k$-by-$1/(k+1)$ rectangles (across $1 \le k$ natural)?
    25Ôćĺ
    26ÔćĺI am deliberately not requiring that the rotations can only be $0^\circ, 90^\circ, 180^\circ, \text{ or } 270^\circ$.
    27Ôćĺ
    28ÔćĺBecause of indexing, since `n : ÔäĽ` starts at 0, we change the side lengths to $1 / (n + 1)$ and
    29Ôćĺ$1 / (n + 2)$, so that the first rectangle is $1/1$ by $1/2$, the second is $1/2$ by $1/3$, etc.
    30Ôćĺ
    31Ôćĺ*Reference:* [mathoverflow/34145](https://mathoverflow.net/q/34145)
    32Ôćĺasked by user [*Kaveh*](https://mathoverflow.net/users/7507/kaveh)
    33Ôćĺ-/
    34Ôćĺ
    35Ôćĺnamespace Mathoverflow34145
    36Ôćĺ
    37Ôćĺ/-- A rectangle is specified by its width, height, starting point, and rotation.
    38ÔćĺThe rectangle is assumed to start in the lower left corner. For example, the unit square
    39Ôćĺ$\{ (x, y) \mid 0 \le x \le 1, 0 \le y \le 1 \}$ is specified as `ÔčĘ1, 1, (0, 0), 0Ôčę`  -/
    40Ôćĺstructure Rectangle : Type where
    41Ôćĺ  width : ÔäŁ
    42Ôćĺ  height : ÔäŁ
    43Ôćĺ  start : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ
    44Ôćĺ  rotation : Angle
    45Ôćĺ
    46Ôćĺ/-- A combination of a rotation and a translation to map the standard rectangle to the desired
    47Ôćĺrectangle.
    48Ôćĺ-/
    49Ôćĺnoncomputable def rigidMotion (start : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) (╬Ş : Angle) (p : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) : (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
    50Ôćĺ  (start.1 + p.1 * ╬Ş.cos - p.2 * ╬Ş.sin, start.2 + p.1 * ╬Ş.sin + p.2 * ╬Ş.cos)
    51Ôćĺ
    52Ôćĺ@[category test, AMS 51]
    53Ôćĺlemma rigidMotion_test : rigidMotion (sqrt 2, sqrt 11) (2 * ¤Ç / 3 : ÔäŁ) (sqrt 5, sqrt 7) =
    54Ôćĺ    (sqrt 2 - sqrt 5 / 2 - sqrt 21 / 2, -sqrt 7 / 2 + sqrt 11 + sqrt 15 / 2) := by
    55Ôćĺ  simp [rigidMotion, mul_div_assoc, cos_two_mul, sin_two_mul,
    56Ôćĺ    show (21 : ÔäŁ) = 3 * 7 by norm_num, show (15 : ÔäŁ) = 3 * 5 by norm_num]
    57Ôćĺ  ring_nf; simp
    58Ôćĺ
    59Ôćĺ/-- A scaling to map the unit square to a standard rectangle. -/
    60Ôćĺnoncomputable def scale (x y : ÔäŁ) (p : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) : (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
    61Ôćĺ  (x * p.1, y * p.2)
    62Ôćĺ
    63Ôćĺ/-- The unit square. -/
    64Ôćĺdef unitSquare : Set (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
    65Ôćĺ  { p | 0 ÔëĄ p.1 Ôłž p.1 ÔëĄ 1 Ôłž 0 ÔëĄ p.2 Ôłž p.2 ÔëĄ 1 }
    66Ôćĺ
    67Ôćĺ/-- Converts a rectangle to a set in `ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ`. -/
    68Ôćĺdef Rectangle.toSet (r : Rectangle) : Set (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
    69Ôćĺ  rigidMotion r.start r.rotation '' (scale r.width r.height '' unitSquare)
    70Ôćĺ
    71Ôćĺ/-- The standard Lebesgue measure on `ÔäŁ┬▓`. -/
    72Ôćĺnoncomputable abbrev lbMeasure : Measure (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
    73Ôćĺ  (Basis.finTwoProd ÔäŁ).addHaar
    74Ôćĺ
    75Ôćĺ/-- `lbMeasure` is invariant under `rigidMotion start ╬Ş`. -/
    76Ôćĺ@[category test, AMS 51]
    77Ôćĺlemma lbMeasure_rigidMotion (start : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) (╬Ş : Angle) (s : Set (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ)) :
    78Ôćĺ    lbMeasure (rigidMotion start ╬Ş '' s) = lbMeasure s := by
    79Ôćĺ  let ╬▒ (x : ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) := (x.1 * ╬Ş.cos - x.2 * ╬Ş.sin, x.1 * ╬Ş.sin + x.2 * ╬Ş.cos)
    80Ôćĺ  trans (Basis.finTwoProd _).addHaar (╬▒ '' s)
    81Ôćĺ  ┬Ě exact (measure_preimage_add_right _ start _).symm.trans (congr_arg _ (Set.ext <| by
    82Ôćĺ      simp [rigidMotion, ╬▒, add_sub_assoc, add_assoc, Prod.ext_iff, ÔćÉ eq_sub_iff_add_eq']))
    83Ôćĺ  ┬Ě let ╬▓ := Basis.finTwoProd ÔäŁ
    84Ôćĺ    trans ╬▓.addHaar (╬▓.constr ÔäŁ ![╬▒ (╬▓ 0), ╬▒ (╬▓ 1)] '' s)
    85Ôćĺ    ┬Ě exact congr_arg _ <| Set.image_congr <| by aesop
    86Ôćĺ    ┬Ě rw [addHaar_image_linearMap, eq_comm, ÔćÉ LinearMap.det_toMatrix ╬▓, Matrix.det_fin_two]
    87Ôćĺ      simp [╬▒, ╬▓, LinearMap.toMatrix_apply, ÔćÉ sq]
    88Ôćĺ
    89Ôćĺ/-- `lbMeasure` is scaled by `scale`. -/
    90Ôćĺ@[category test, AMS 51]
    91Ôćĺlemma lbMeasure_scale (x y : ÔäŁ) (s : Set (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ)) :
    92Ôćĺ    lbMeasure (scale x y '' s) = .ofReal |x * y| * lbMeasure s := by
    93Ôćĺ  let scaleLinear : (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) ÔćĺÔéŚ[ÔäŁ] (ÔäŁ ├Ś ÔäŁ) :=
    94Ôćĺ  { toFun p := (x * p.1, y * p.2)
    95Ôćĺ    map_add' p q := by simp [mul_add]
    96Ôćĺ    map_smul' c p := by simp [mul_left_comm] }
    97Ôćĺ  have hÔéü : scale x y = scaleLinear := rfl
    98Ôćĺ  have hÔéé : scaleLinear.toMatrix (Basis.finTwoProd ÔäŁ) (Basis.finTwoProd ÔäŁ) = !![x, 0; 0, y] := by
    99Ôćĺ    ext i j; unfold scaleLinear; fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> simp [LinearMap.toMatrix_apply]
   100Ôćĺ  simp [hÔéü, ÔćÉ LinearMap.det_toMatrix (Basis.finTwoProd ÔäŁ), hÔéé]
   101Ôćĺ
   102Ôćĺ/-- The Lebesgue measure of the unit square is `1`. -/
   103Ôćĺ@[category test, AMS 51]
   104Ôćĺlemma lbMeasure_unitSquare : lbMeasure unitSquare = 1 := by
   105Ôćĺ  convert (Basis.addHaar_eq_iff (Basis.finTwoProd ÔäŁ) _).1 rfl
   106Ôćĺ  ext p
   107Ôćĺ  simp only [unitSquare, Set.mem_setOf_eq, Basis.coe_parallelepiped, mem_parallelepiped_iff,
   108Ôćĺ    Set.mem_Icc, Fin.sum_univ_two, Fin.isValue, Basis.finTwoProd_zero, Prod.smul_mk, smul_eq_mul,
   109Ôćĺ    mul_one, mul_zero, Basis.finTwoProd_one, Prod.mk_add_mk, add_zero, zero_add, Pi.le_def]
   110Ôćĺ  exact ÔčĘfun h ÔćŽ ÔčĘ![p.1, p.2], by simp [Fin.forall_fin_succ, h]Ôčę,
   111Ôćĺ    fun ÔčĘt, htÔčę ÔćŽ ht.2 ÔľŞ ÔčĘht.1.1 0, ht.1.2 0, ht.1.1 1, ht.1.2 1ÔčęÔčę
   112Ôćĺ
   113Ôćĺ/-- The Lebesgue measure of the a rectangle `r` is `r.width * r.height` -/
   114Ôćĺ@[category test, AMS 51]
   115Ôćĺlemma lbMeasure_rectangle_toSet (r : Rectangle) :
   116Ôćĺ    lbMeasure r.toSet = .ofReal |r.width * r.height| := by
   117Ôćĺ  rw [Rectangle.toSet, lbMeasure_rigidMotion, lbMeasure_scale, lbMeasure_unitSquare, mul_one]
   118Ôćĺ
   119Ôćĺ/-- The areas of the required rectangles sum to 1. -/
   120Ôćĺ@[category test, AMS 51]
   121Ôćĺlemma tsum_area_eq_one : ÔłĹ' (n : ÔäĽ), ((1 / (n + 1)) * (1 / (n + 2)) : ÔäŁ) = 1 := by
   122Ôćĺ  have (n : ÔäĽ) : ÔłĹ i Ôłł Finset.range n, (1 / (i + 1) * (1 / (i + 2)) : ÔäŁ) = 1 - 1 / (n + 1) := by
   123Ôćĺ    induction n with
   124Ôćĺ    | zero => simp
   125Ôćĺ    | succ n ih => rw [Finset.sum_range_succ, ih]; field_simp; push_cast; ring
   126Ôćĺ  refine HasSum.tsum_eq ((hasSum_iff_tendsto_nat_of_nonneg (fun i ÔćŽ ?_) _).2 ?_)
   127Ôćĺ  ┬Ě positivity
   128Ôćĺ  ┬Ě simp_rw [this]
   129Ôćĺ    convert tendsto_one_div_add_atTop_nhds_zero_nat.const_sub (1 : ÔäŁ) (c := 0) using 1
   130Ôćĺ    norm_num
   131Ôćĺ
   132Ôćĺ/-- A configuration of rectangles of sides `1 / (n + 1)` and `1 / (n + 2)`. -/
   133Ôćĺstructure Configuration : Type where
   134Ôćĺ  rect (n : ÔäĽ) : Rectangle
   135Ôćĺ  rect_width (n : ÔäĽ) : (rect n).width = 1 / (n + 1)
   136Ôćĺ  rect_height (n : ÔäĽ) : (rect n).height = 1 / (n + 2)
   137Ôćĺ
   138Ôćĺ/-- A "packing" means that the interiors of any two rectangles are disjoint. -/
   139Ôćĺdef Configuration.IsPacking (c : Configuration) : Prop :=
   140Ôćĺ  Pairwise fun m n ÔćŽ interior (c.rect m).toSet Ôłę interior (c.rect n).toSet = Ôłů
   141Ôćĺ
   142Ôćĺ/-- Can a unit square be covered by rectangles of width `1 / (n + 1)` and height `1 / (n + 2)`? -/
   143Ôćĺ@[category research open, AMS 51]
   144Ôćĺtheorem rectangles_cover_unit_square :
   145Ôćĺ    answer(sorry) Ôćö Ôłâ c : Configuration, ÔłÇ p Ôłł unitSquare, Ôłâ n, p Ôłł (c.rect n).toSet := by
   146Ôćĺ  sorry
   147Ôćĺ
   148Ôćĺ/-- Equivalently, can a unit square be packed with rectangles of width `1 / (n + 1)` and height
   149Ôćĺ`1 / (n + 2)`? -/
   150Ôćĺ@[category research open, AMS 51]
   151Ôćĺtheorem rectangles_pack_unit_square :
   152Ôćĺ    answer(sorry) Ôćö Ôłâ c : Configuration, (ÔłÇ n, (c.rect n).toSet ÔŐć unitSquare) Ôłž c.IsPacking := by
   153Ôćĺ  sorry
   154Ôćĺ
   155Ôćĺ/-- It is known that packing the rectangles into a square of side length `133/132` is possible.
   156Ôćĺ
   157ÔćĺReference: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0097316594901163
   158Ôćĺ-/
   159Ôćĺ@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   160Ôćĺtheorem rectangles_pack_square_133_div_132 :
   161Ôćĺ    (Ôłâ c : Configuration,
   162Ôćĺ      (ÔłÇ n, (c.rect n).toSet ÔŐć Rectangle.toSet ÔčĘ133/132, 133/132, (0, 0), 0Ôčę) Ôłž
   163Ôćĺ      c.IsPacking) := by
   164Ôćĺ  sorry
   165Ôćĺ
   166Ôćĺ/-- It is known that packing the rectangles into a square of side length `501/500` is possible.
   167Ôćĺ
   168ÔćĺReference: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0167506008706009
   169Ôćĺ-/
   170Ôćĺ@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   171Ôćĺtheorem rectangles_pack_square_501_div_500 :
   172Ôćĺ    (Ôłâ c : Configuration,
   173Ôćĺ      (ÔłÇ n, (c.rect n).toSet ÔŐć Rectangle.toSet ÔčĘ501/500, 501/500, (0, 0), 0Ôčę) Ôłž
   174Ôćĺ      c.IsPacking) := by
   175Ôćĺ  sorry
   176Ôćĺ
   177Ôćĺend Mathoverflow34145
   178Ôćĺ

Can the unit square be covered by $1/k$-by-$1/(k+1)$ rectangles (across $1 \le k$ natural)?

I am deliberately not requiring that the rotations can only be $0^\circ, 90^\circ, 180^\circ, \text{ or } 270^\circ$.

asked by user [*Kaveh*](https://mathoverflow.net/users/7507/kaveh)

namespace Mathoverflow34145
